title: Thickness-driven crossover from conventional to chiral nonreciprocal superconductivity in kagome metal CsV3Sb5
authors: Wei Zhang, Jiangbo Luo, Nikolai Peshcherenko, Zheyu Wang, Chun Wai Tsang, Kwing To Lai, King Yau Yip, Kenji Watanabe, Takashi Taniguchi, Junxiong Hu, Yang Zhang, Swee K. Goh, A. Ariando
abstract: Superconductivity and its potential applications are governed by the symmetry of the superconducting order parameter. In the kagome metal CsV3Sb5, most bulk studies indicate conventional s-wave pairing. However, ultrathin flakes exhibit nonreciprocal transport, in particular a zero-field superconducting diode effect, which requires broken inversion and time-reversal symmetries. Here, using thickness dependent transport measurements, we observe the emergence of non-reciprocal second-harmonic magnetotransport signals and a zero-field superconducting diode effect, accompanied by a pronounced reduction of the out-of-plane coherence length with decreasing thickness. Upper critical field measurements further reveal a dimensional crossover from three-dimensional superconductivity in bulk to two-dimensional superconductivity in thin flakes. These findings indicate a thickness-induced chiral superconducting phase that breaks both inversion and time-reversal symmetries in the two-dimensional limit. Our work not only clarifies long-standing controversies regarding the pairing symmetry in CsV3Sb5, but also establishes thin-flake kagome superconductors as a versatile platform for engineering nonreciprocal quantum devices and exploring emergent topological phases.
summary: This work investigates the superconducting state of the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 by examining the effect of sample thickness. The authors demonstrate that as the material is thinned below a critical thickness, the superconducting pairing symmetry changes from conventional to a chiral state. This transition is evidenced by the emergence of non-reciprocal transport and a zero-field superconducting diode effect, establishing thin flakes as a platform for topological quantum devices.
main_problem: The study aims to resolve the pairing symmetry in the kagome metal CsV3Sb5 and understand how superconductivity evolves as the sample thickness is reduced.
main_result: The superconducting state undergoes a thickness-induced crossover from a conventional 3D s-wave pairing in the bulk to a 2D chiral superconducting phase that spontaneously breaks both inversion and time-reversal symmetries in ultrathin flakes.
method: The research combines thickness-dependent transport measurements, zero-field superconducting diode effect (SDE) measurements, and upper critical field anisotropy studies.
model_or_system: The physical system is the kagome metal CsV3Sb5, studied in bulk form versus exfoliated ultrathin flakes. The theoretical analysis models the superconducting state using Ginzburg-Landau functionals to predict non-reciprocal behavior and $s+is$ pairing.
key_observables: Nonreciprocal second-harmonic magnetotransport signals, zero-field superconducting diode effect (SDE), upper critical field anisotropy ($\Gamma$), and out-of-plane coherence length ($\xi_c$).
important_parameters: Thickness (critical transition around 100 nm), magnetic field ($H$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The non-reciprocal effects and SDE are fundamentally linked to the dimensional crossover and the breaking of P and T symmetries in the 2D limit.
figures_summary: Figures show the systematic disappearance of nonreciprocity and SDE as flakes become thicker, alongside data demonstrating the crossover of $H_{c2}$ anisotropy from 3D to 2D behavior as thickness decreases.
paper_structure: The paper systematically compares bulk measurements (suggesting s-wave) with measurements on increasingly thin flakes, using transport diagnostics (SDE, nonreciprocity) and anisotropy analysis ($H_{c2}$, $\xi_c$) to build a case for a thickness-driven chiral superconducting phase.
why_interesting: The observation of spontaneous time-reversal symmetry breaking and chiral superconductivity in a layered material via thickness control provides a clean platform for studying topological superconductivity, relevant to advanced quantum device engineering.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
